title: How to handle the paradox of wanting connection while fearing it
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

The war inside your own body 

 You want connection so badly it hurts. You can feel it in your chest, that hollow ache when you see couples laughing at a...
htmlContent:
The war inside your own body

You want connection so badly it hurts. You can feel it in your chest, that hollow ache when you see couples laughing at a cafe, when you scroll past photos of friends at dinner parties you weren't invited to, when you lie awake at 2 AM and wonder if anyone would notice if you just disappeared. But then someone reaches out. A text comes in. An invitation lands in your inbox. And your body locks up. Your throat tightens. Your stomach drops. Suddenly you're exhausted, or busy, or sick, or any of the thousand excuses you've perfected over the years.

Here's the thing. This isn't a character flaw. This isn't you being broken or difficult or "too much." This is your nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do. Keep you alive. Keep you safe. Keep you from getting hurt again.

I know this because I've lived it. For years I thought something was wrong with me. I'd meet someone wonderful, someone who actually saw me, and I'd run. I'd find reasons they were wrong for me. I'd pick fights over nothing. I'd ghost. I'd disappear. And then I'd hate myself for disappearing, which only made me more convinced that I didn't deserve connection anyway. It's a vicious loop. A trap with no visible exit.

But here's what I've learned. The exit isn't about forcing yourself to trust people. It's about understanding what's happening in your body when connection becomes terrifying. And it starts with the science.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, explains this better than anyone. He says our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. Not just physical danger, but relational danger. The kind of danger that comes from being rejected, abandoned, shamed, or betrayed. And when your nervous system has learned, through experience, that connection leads to pain, it will do everything in its power to keep you away from it.

Stay with me here. This is the key. Your fear of connection isn't a weakness. It's a survival strategy that once worked. Maybe it protected you from an unpredictable parent. Maybe it kept you safe from a partner who hurt you. Maybe it was the only way you could survive a childhood where love came with conditions, or with violence, or with silence. Your nervous system isn't stupid. It's just stuck. Stuck in a pattern that's no longer serving you.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about how trauma disrupts the basic sense of connection to others. And that disruption doesn't just go away because you want it to. It requires re-learning, at the level of the body, that connection can be safe.

Why your brain says yes but your body says no

You've probably experienced this. You make a plan to meet a friend. You're excited. You genuinely want to go. But the morning of, you wake up with a knot in your stomach. Your head hurts. Your jaw is tight. You start finding reasons to cancel. The traffic will be bad. You're too tired. You have too much work. And you know, on some level, that these are lies. But the relief you feel when you cancel is real. It's physical. It's a release.

That relief is your nervous system celebrating. It just prevented you from entering a situation it perceives as dangerous. And here's the cruel irony. The danger it's protecting you from is the very thing you need to heal. Connection. Intimacy. Being seen.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. He talks about how the brain's alarm system gets stuck in overdrive. Even when the threat is gone, your body still reacts as if it's present. So when someone reaches for you with kindness, your body doesn't register kindness. It registers threat. It prepares for the inevitable betrayal, the abandonment, the pain that always follows closeness.

Think about it. When was the last time someone genuinely complimented you and you believed them? Not deflected. Not minimized. Not found a way to twist it into something negative. If you're like me, that's rare. Almost impossible. Because your nervous system has learned that compliments are often followed by criticism. That closeness is followed by distance. That love is followed by loss.

I want you to notice something. Right now, as you're reading this, what's happening in your body? Is your chest tight? Are your shoulders hunched? Is your breath shallow? Don't try to change it. Just notice. That's your nervous system doing its job. Protecting you from the vulnerability of this moment, from the possibility that what I'm saying might actually mean something to you.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. He developed Internal Family Systems therapy, and his core insight is that there are no bad parts of you. Not the part that pushes people away. Not the part that sabotages relationships. Not the part that isolates. These parts are trying to protect you. They just don't know that you're not in danger anymore.

The slow work of teaching your body it's safe

So what do you do? How do you handle this paradox? How do you want connection and fear it at the same time, without going crazy?

First, you stop trying to resolve the paradox. You stop trying to make it go away. The paradox isn't a problem to solve. It's a reality to be with. You can want connection AND fear it. Both things are true. Neither invalidates the other. And the more you fight the fear, the stronger it gets. The more you shame yourself for wanting connection, the more disconnected you feel.

Second, you start small. Ridiculously small. Not "go to a party and make friends" small. I mean "make eye contact with the barista and say thank you" small. I mean "send a one-sentence text to a friend and don't wait for a reply" small. I mean "sit in a coffee shop for ten minutes and let yourself be around other people without interacting" small.

This is called titration in trauma therapy. It means approaching the edge of your window of tolerance without falling out of it. You touch the fear, but you don't let it overwhelm you. You feel the pull toward connection, but you don't force yourself to jump into the deep end. You take one step. Then you pause. Then you check in with your body. Then you take another step.

Wild, right? The way to heal from isolation isn't to force yourself to connect. It's to slowly, gently, repeatedly show your nervous system that connection can be safe. That not every hand that reaches for you is going to hurt you. That not every kind word is a setup for betrayal.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Arielle Schwartz. It has exercises for tracking your nervous system states and building capacity for connection at your own pace. I've used it myself. It's not about fixing yourself. It's about understanding yourself.

What to do when the fear wins

Let's be honest. Sometimes the fear wins. You cancel. You ghost. You isolate. You tell yourself you'll try again tomorrow, and tomorrow never comes. And then you hate yourself for it. You spiral into shame. You tell yourself you're broken, that you'll never heal, that you deserve to be alone.

I need you to hear something. That shame spiral is also a survival strategy. It's your nervous system's way of keeping you small, keeping you safe, keeping you from taking risks. Because if you believe you're broken, you won't try. And if you don't try, you can't fail. And if you can't fail, you can't be hurt.

But here's the truth. The shame is a liar. The belief that you're broken is a protection, not a fact. And the only way out is through. Not through the fear, necessarily. But through the shame about the fear.

When you cancel on a friend, instead of spiraling into self-hatred, try this. Put your hand on your chest. Take three slow breaths. And say to yourself: "I understand why I did that. My body was trying to protect me. I'm not bad for being scared. I'm human."

It sounds ridiculous. I know. But try it. Right now. Notice what happens in your body when you offer yourself that kind of compassion. Does your chest soften? Do your shoulders drop? Do you feel a little less alone?

That softening is your nervous system beginning to trust you. Beginning to believe that you're not going to force it into danger. That you're on its side. That you're not going to abandon yourself the way others have.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson. It explains why connection felt so dangerous in the first place. For many of us, our parents couldn't handle our emotions. So we learned that being close meant being invisible, or being responsible for someone else's feelings. That pattern doesn't just go away. But naming it is the first step.

You don't have to fix this alone

I'm not going to tell you that healing is linear. It's not. Some days you'll feel brave and connected and alive. Other days you'll want to crawl under your blankets and never speak to anyone again. Both are okay. Both are part of the process.

I'm also not going to tell you that you need to forgive everyone who hurt you, or that you need to "open your heart" and "let love in." Those are toxic positivity phrases that ignore the real pain of relational trauma. You don't have to forgive anyone. You don't have to let anyone in before you're ready. You just have to keep yourself. Keep noticing what your body needs. Keep practicing, in tiny ways, being seen.

And please, if you can, find a therapist who understands trauma. Not just any therapist. Someone trained in somatic approaches, or EMDR, or Internal Family Systems, or Sensorimotor Psychotherapy. Someone who understands that your fear of connection isn't a character flaw but a survival response. Someone who can sit with you in the paradox without trying to fix it.

You are not alone in this. The paradox of wanting connection while fearing it is one of the most universal experiences of complex trauma. It's not a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that you've survived. And survival, as hard as it is, is the foundation of healing.

You don't have to have it figured out. You just have to keep going. One small step. One moment of noticing. One breath of compassion. Over and over again. That's how you handle the paradox. Not by resolving it. But by learning to live with it, and in the process, learning to live with yourself.

And maybe, eventually, learning to let someone else live with you too.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Arielle Schwartz
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson
